title: Linked List
description: Building the real structure behind STL's list by hand: a Node struct, pointers connecting them, and manual traversal.
objectives: Define a Node struct with data and a next pointer
Build a linked list by hand using new
Traverse a linked list using a pointer
Understand what list was actually doing internally all along

sections.text: 1. Recap: STL Gave Us list — Now Build the Real Thing
list handed us push_front(), push_back(), and iterators, without showing what's actually happening underneath. Now, using Pointers, new, and a simple struct, we build the real structure ourselves.
2. What is a Linked List, Structurally?
A linked list is a sequence of nodes, each storing a value and a pointer to the next node, chained together without needing contiguous memory the way an array does.

struct works like class from Classes and Objects, but with public members by default (recall the class-vs-struct default from Access Specifiers). Node *next is the crucial piece: each node holds the address of the next one, exactly the pointer mechanics from Pointers.
4. Building a Simple Linked List by Hand

sections.code:
Node *head = new Node{1, nullptr}; // first node, created on the heap (recall new)
head->next = new Node{2, nullptr}; // second node, linked from the first
head->next->next = new Node{3, nullptr}; // third node, linked from the second

sections.text: head->next (recall -> from this Pointer, accessing a member through a pointer) is what actually forms the chain. Each new Node lives independently on the heap (recall Heap Memory) — nothing requires them to sit next to each other in memory, unlike an array.

sections.title: Tracing it
points: current starts at head (the node holding 1)
Print current->data, then move: current = current->next
This repeats until current becomes nullptr — the natural 'end of the list' marker, since the last node's next was never set to point anywhere
This while (current != nullptr) traversal is precisely what list's begin()/end() iterators do automatically underneath

sections.text: Recall list's trade-offs from earlier: no [] indexing, but cheap insertion/removal anywhere, because relinking a couple of next pointers is all that's needed — no shifting like an array. Now that trade-off is visible directly: inserting a node just means creating one new Node and rewiring two next pointers, never touching the other nodes at all.

sections.title: A scavenger hunt, one clue per box
sections.text: Recall list's treasure hunt analogy — this is that treasure hunt, made literal. Each box (Node) holds a prize (data) and a note telling you which box to open next (next). The boxes don't need to be sitting next to each other on a shelf; a note can point anywhere. Reaching a box with no further note (nullptr) means the hunt is over.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting to initialize a new node's next to nullptr — an uninitialized pointer is dangerous to dereference later, exactly the garbage-value risk from Variables applied to pointers.
Losing the head pointer while traversing — always traverse with a separate variable (like current), never overwrite head itself, or the list becomes unreachable.
Forgetting the loop-termination condition current != nullptr — without it, traversal runs past the last real node into undefined memory.
Confusing . and -> — a Node object uses ., but current, being a pointer to a Node, must use -> to reach its members.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What are the two pieces of data a linked list node typically holds?
questions.answer: The actual value (data) and a pointer to the next node in the chain (next).

questions.question: Why doesn't a linked list need contiguous memory, unlike an array?
questions.answer: Because each node is independently allocated (commonly on the heap with new) and connected purely through pointers, rather than relying on physical adjacency in memory.

questions.question: How do you know when you've reached the end of a linked list while traversing it?
questions.answer: The current pointer becomes nullptr, since the last node's next was never set to point to another node.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A linked list is built from Node structs, each holding a value and a next pointer to the following node, connected with new and traversed with ->, exactly the pointer mechanics from earlier lessons. Unlike an array, nodes don't need to be stored contiguously — each is independently allocated, commonly on the heap. Traversal walks a separate pointer (current) from the head, printing each node's data and following next, until reaching nullptr, the natural end-of-list marker. This is exactly what STL's list container was doing automatically underneath its begin()/end() iterators all along, and it's what makes insertion/removal cheap: just rewiring a couple of next pointers, never shifting other nodes.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Linked List
sections.text: Node = a box with a prize (data) and a note pointing to the next box (next). nullptr = the hunt ends here. -> reaches through a pointer to its box's contents.
